tabDescriptions.punctuation: Punctuations are characters that are used in texts so the readability of texts become better. Punctuation sometimes also indicates how a sentence should be pronounced, or what a word or sentence means.
tabDescriptions.currencies: Several currencies have their own HTML character which you can use on your website. Find the HTML character from the HTML currency code list below.
tabDescriptions.mathematical: There are quite a few mathematical HTML characters which you can use on your website. This list of HTML mathematical characters will help you find the right mathematical character to display in your HTML.
tabDescriptions.pronunciations: Pronunciation characters will help the readers of a text understand how a word should be spoken. Below you'll find all HTML pronunciation characters which you can use in your HTML.
tabDescriptions.diacritics: There are a lot of letters and characters that have a glyph or accent added to the letter or character. These diacritics can make the pronunciation or meaning of a word different. This HTML diacritics list will allow you to display the right diacritic on your website.
tabDescriptions.ascii: The ASCII (American Standard Code for Information Interchange) subset of Unicode includes the numbers, letters and characters which are basically the most-known characters. The ASCII subset includes the numbers 0 to 9, the lower case letters a to z, upper case letters A to Z and some basic punctuation and control codes. The following ASCII HTML list includes all ASCII subset Unicode HTML codes.
tabDescriptions.icons: HTML also offers special characters which you can use in your HTML. For example, a heart, a gender icon, musical notes or arrows. Use the special characters and icons from the below HTML icon list and special characters freely on your website.

searchPlaceholder: Search by character, code, or description...

descriptions.p1: HTML entities are special codes used to represent reserved characters in HTML. For example, < for <, & for &, and " for ". See the [ASCII table](/ascii) for character codes, or [HTTP status codes](/httpstatus) for web response references.
descriptions.p2: Before you use one of the below codes on your website, make sure your HTML document uses the correct encoding so you are sure that the HTML codes and HTML special codes are displayed properly. To make sure your HTML uses the correct encoding, you should make sure that you use the Unicode character set. This Unicode character set must be set in the head section of your HTML document. This is done by using a meta with the charset attribute and the value UTF-8. Just copy and paste the following meta into your head section and all the below HTML characters will be displayed perfectly so you're good to go.
descriptions.aeoDefinition: HTML Entity Encoder is a free online tool for encoding and decoding HTML special characters and entities. Complete reference with numeric and named entities. Runs entirely in your browser.
descriptions.whatIs: HTML entities are codes that represent characters reserved in HTML (like <, >, &) or characters that cannot be typed easily. They use the format &name; or &#number; — for example, < renders as < and © renders as the copyright symbol. This tool provides a complete reference of named entities, decimal codes, and hex codes across 8 categories.
descriptions.useCasesP1: Escape reserved HTML characters like <, >, &, and " so they display as text instead of being parsed as markup.
descriptions.useCasesP2: Insert special symbols like currency signs, mathematical operators, or typographic marks into web pages using their entity codes.
descriptions.step1Text: Select a tab — Letters, Punctuation, Currencies, Mathematical, Pronunciations, Diacritics, ASCII, or Icons.
descriptions.step2Text: Use the search bar to filter by character, entity name, or code point. Or scroll through the full table.
descriptions.step3Text: Each row shows the character, named entity, decimal code, and hex code. Copy whichever format your project needs.
descriptions.faq1A: The tool provides a complete reference of HTML special characters including named entities (&), decimal (&), and hexadecimal (&) codes.
descriptions.faq2Q: Can I search for specific characters?
descriptions.faq2A: Yes. Search by character name, entity name, or code point to quickly find the HTML entity you need.
descriptions.faq3Q: What is the difference between named and numeric entities?
descriptions.faq3A: Named entities use a mnemonic like & for & or < for <. Numeric entities use the Unicode code point: & (decimal) or & (hex). Named entities are more readable, but numeric entities work for all Unicode characters.
